findings.summary: Private `core._internal` utilities are consumed cross-layer, collapsing intended boundaries.
evidence: `text_utils.py` explicitly documents legacy compatibility and runtime-root semantics, but external layers import it directly instead of public APIs.
App-level files import `get_project_root`/`get_area` from `_internal`, bypassing `core/paths_api.py` and making internal implementation details part of de facto public contracts.
This pattern repeats across engine/languages (`rg` shows many direct `_internal` imports), indicating systemic boundary erosion rather than isolated usage.
findings.suggestion: Create explicit public utility modules (`core/paths_api`, `core/value_utils`, etc.) for all externally used helpers, migrate non-core imports off `_internal`, and enforce with an import-linter rule that only `desloppify.core.*` may import `desloppify.core._internal.*`.

findings.summary: Review command package wiring creates cycle-prone imports through package root.
evidence: `review/__init__.py` imports `cmd_review` from `entrypoint` at import time.
`entrypoint.py` imports `_do_run_batches` from `batch.py`.
`batch.py` imports `batch_core`, `batches`, and `runner_helpers` via `from desloppify.app.commands.review import ...`, forcing package-level resolution while package initialization is in flight.
findings.suggestion: Break package-root import recursion: avoid importing `entrypoint` in `review/__init__.py` (or make it lazy via `__getattr__`), and in `batch.py` import siblings directly (module-relative) without package-root re-entry. Keep command registration at CLI layer instead of package import side effects.

findings.summary: Canonical finding-status semantics are duplicated as raw strings across command modules.
evidence: Multiple modules hardcode status tokens and status-group logic (`"open"`, `"wontfix"`, `"auto_resolved"`, etc.) in local filters and UI mapping.
`core/enums.py` already defines canonical status tokens/normalization, but command-level code repeatedly redefines subsets and behavior.
This makes status-policy changes cross-cutting and brittle, increasing coupling between rendering/orchestration and state semantics.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single status-policy service (e.g., `core/status_policy.py`) exposing actionable status sets and presentation metadata; migrate render/plan/resolve modules to consume it instead of local string tuples/maps.

findings.summary: Current queue and preflight policies can indefinitely starve subjective review improvements.
evidence: `review/preflight.py` blocks reruns when any objective backlog exists for already-scored dimensions unless `--force-review-rerun` is used.
`_work_queue/ranking.py` forces subjective items to tier 4 and explicitly keeps them behind mechanical T1/T2/T3.
Combined policy can trap teams in objective-only churn while subjective dimensions remain stale and continue to cap strict progress.
findings.suggestion: Add fairness guardrails: allow periodic subjective reruns when subjective drag dominates (or after N scans), and add queue-budget rules (e.g., reserve 1 subjective slot per K resolved mechanical items). Implement in `review/preflight.py` and `engine/_work_queue/ranking.py` with explicit policy knobs in config.

findings.summary: Language plugin discovery latches after first load and cannot be cleanly refreshed in-process
evidence: `registry_state.clear()` only calls `_registry.clear()` and does not reset `_load_attempted`/`_load_errors`.
`discovery.load_all()` exits early whenever `was_load_attempted()` is true, then only reports prior errors.
`resolution.get_lang/available_langs/auto_detect_lang` all call `load_all()`, so later calls cannot force rediscovery once latched.
findings.suggestion: Replace module globals with a `LanguageRegistry` object exposing atomic `reset()` and `load_all(force_reload=False)` semantics; ensure clear/reset paths reset attempted/error flags together and wire CLI/plugin-update flows to explicit reload when needed.

findings.summary: Runtime detector registration can diverge from CLI cached detector list
evidence: `core/registry.register_detector()` mutates global `DETECTORS` and supports callbacks.
`cli._get_detector_names()` memoizes `_DETECTOR_NAMES` once with no invalidation hook.
Narrative constants subscribe to `on_detector_registered`, but CLI cache does not, so subsystem behavior depends on registration timing.
findings.suggestion: Either remove CLI detector-name memoization or subscribe CLI cache invalidation to `on_detector_registered`; alternatively centralize detector reads behind a registry snapshot API with versioning.

findings.summary: Review packet-preparation logic is duplicated across prepare and batch command paths
evidence: Both modules define identical `DEFAULT_REVIEW_BATCH_MAX_FILES = 80` and near-identical `_coerce_review_batch_file_limit` and `_redacted_review_config` helpers.
Both paths perform overlapping holistic packet preparation concerns (dimensions parsing, retrospective coercion, `prepare_holistic_review` invocation, next-command construction).
This duplication already diverges in small behavior details (`--scan-after-import` handling), indicating drift pressure.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `review_packet_service` (input coercion, config redaction, packet assembly, next-command synthesis) and make `do_prepare`/`_load_or_prepare_packet` thin adapters.

findings.summary: Two separate finding-merge implementations duplicate and can drift semantically
evidence: `review/merge.py` `_merge_finding_details` merges `related_files`, `evidence`, and suggestion using custom precedence.
`review/batch_core.py` `_merge_finding_payload` reimplements similar merging with partly different precedence (`summary` + `suggestion`), separate identifier handling, and similar dedupe loops.
Maintaining two merge policies for the same finding shape increases inconsistent output/import behavior across command flows.
findings.suggestion: Unify into one shared finding-merge utility with explicit policy flags (interactive merge vs batch aggregation) and test shared invariants once.

findings.summary: Plan override handlers perform split state/plan writes without transaction boundary
evidence: `cmd_plan_skip` writes state resolution first (`state_mod.save_state`) then plan skip metadata (`save_plan`), with no rollback if second write fails.
`cmd_plan_unskip`/`cmd_plan_reopen` similarly coordinate state + plan updates manually in handler code.
The same two-store choreography is repeated in multiple handlers, signaling a missing abstraction for consistency guarantees.
findings.suggestion: Introduce an application-level transactional helper for coordinated state+plan mutations (apply both or fail with compensating rollback), and move skip/unskip/reopen orchestration into that service.

findings.summary: Project-root resolution contract is inconsistent between runtime-aware and import-time paths
evidence: `text_utils.get_project_root()` is runtime-context aware, but `text_utils.read_code_snippet()` falls back to `PROJECT_ROOT` constant when no explicit root is passed.
`config.CONFIG_FILE` is computed from `PROJECT_ROOT` once at import time, while other path APIs resolve root dynamically.
Commands that rely on default config pathing (`load_config()` with no explicit path) can diverge from runtime-root behavior expected elsewhere.
findings.suggestion: Remove import-time root/path singletons for mutable runtime concerns. Resolve config/snippet paths via `get_project_root()` at call time (or pass explicit root through command runtime) and keep `PROJECT_ROOT` only as legacy read-only compatibility alias.

findings.summary: Assessment score clamping assumes numeric input and can crash on malformed payloads
evidence: `store_assessments()` does `max(0, min(100, score))` without verifying `score` is numeric when sourced from `value_obj.get('score', 0)`.
Payload parsing guarantees `assessments` is a dict, but does not enforce numeric `score` value types before import execution.
A payload like `{ "assessments": {"naming_quality": {"score": "90"}} }` reaches the clamp and raises `TypeError`.
findings.suggestion: Normalize assessment scores with explicit numeric coercion (`float(...)` guarded by `is_numeric`) and fallback behavior; reject invalid assessment payloads with user-facing validation errors before mutation.

findings.summary: Review-merge logic can collapse distinct findings solely on reused identifier token
evidence: `_same_issue_concept()` returns `True` immediately when parsed identifier tokens match, skipping summary/file similarity checks.
Holistic import validation requires `identifier` non-empty but does not enforce uniqueness across findings in a payload.
Merge command then marks duplicates as `auto_resolved`, so reused identifiers can produce false merges and hide unresolved issues.
findings.suggestion: Require identifier match plus at least one corroborating signal (high summary similarity and/or related-file overlap), and enforce per-import identifier uniqueness during holistic import validation.

findings.summary: Review batch runtime policy is duplicated across modules instead of using one policy abstraction
evidence: `batches.py` manually parses/coerces timeout/retry/stall knobs (lines ~330-369) and computes workers/worst-case runtime.
`runtime/policy.py` defines a canonical `BatchRunPolicy` with the same fields and coercion logic but is not used by `batches.py`.
CLI defaults are declared in parser layer while execution defaults are also embedded in orchestration/policy code paths, creating drift pressure.
findings.suggestion: Make `batches.py` consume `resolve_batch_run_policy(args)` as the single source of runtime policy truth; delete local coercion helpers for batch knobs there; keep parser defaults only as input defaults and validate behavior with `pytest desloppify/tests/review -k batch`.

findings.summary: Import pipeline weakens `Required` TypedDict guarantees by optionalizing required payload fields
evidence: `ReviewImportPayload` declares `findings`, `assessments`, `reviewed_files`, `review_scope`, `provenance`, and `dimension_notes` as `Required` fields.
`_normalize_import_payload_shape` reads those keys with `.get(...)` and silently fills empty defaults, so missing required keys are normalized instead of rejected by type-boundary expectations.
findings.suggestion: Align runtime behavior with the typed contract: enforce presence of required top-level keys in one strict validator, then pass a fully-typed `ReviewImportPayload` downstream without fallback defaults; update callers/tests to fail fast on missing keys.

findings.summary: High-fan-in queue/output helpers are not directly exercised by targeted tests
evidence: Holistic testing signals list these modules as critical untested with multiple importers.
Repository test search found no direct references to queue-progress helper entrypoints (`plan_aware_queue_count`, `print_execution_or_reveal`).
`queue_progress.py` controls user-facing score/queue behavior and branch decisions (plan-aware vs live score path), so missing focused tests increases regression risk.
findings.suggestion: Add focused unit tests for queue-progress/output behavior: 1) plan-active frozen score path, 2) plan-cleared live score path, 3) queue-count error fallback, 4) table formatting and no-color behavior; run `pytest desloppify/tests/commands -k "queue or output"` and wire into CI.

findings.summary: The review command package is structurally overloaded and mixes unrelated responsibilities
evidence: Directory profile shows `app/commands/review/` as 16 files and ~6954 LOC with high fan-out and several very large modules.
Execution orchestration, external session lifecycle, trust policy, payload normalization, and scoring/merge logic are interleaved in the same package boundary.
findings.suggestion: Stage a reorg: 1) create `review/orchestration/` (move `batches.py`, `runner_helpers.py`, `batch_core.py`), `review/importing/` (move `import_helpers.py`, `import_cmd.py`, `merge.py`), and `review/external/` (move `external.py`, `runtime/*`); 2) move internal symbols first with compatibility re-exports in old paths; 3) update imports via `rg "app\.commands\.review\.(batches|runner_helpers|import_helpers|external)" -l | xargs sed -i '' ...`; 4) remove shims after tests pass with `pytest desloppify/tests/review desloppify/tests/commands`.

findings.summary: Core review orchestrators combine too many responsibilities in single modules
evidence: Mechanical signal-density highlights `runner_helpers.py` and `batch_core.py` as multi-detector hotspots; `batches.py` is also a complexity hotspot.
`runner_helpers.py` spans process control, stream draining, timeout/stall handling, packet sanitization, provenance building, and follow-up scanning in one file.
`batches.py` performs preflight notices, runtime policy, runner execution, result merge, import dispatch, and scan chaining in one command function.
findings.suggestion: Split orchestration by phase contract (`prepare`, `execute`, `merge`, `import`, `post_scan`) and pass typed phase results between modules; add phase-level tests and keep `cmd_review`/`_do_run_batches` as thin composition layers only.

findings.summary: Sibling-convention detection collapses the codebase into one bucket, producing noisy outliers.
evidence: `_sibling_behavior_context` buckets by top-level segment (`parts[0]/`) when `base_path` is set, so most repository files are analyzed as one sibling group.
The packet’s `sibling_behavior` has only `desloppify/` and marks many boilerplate files as outliers for missing `annotations`.
`_batch_conventions_errors` consumes these outliers directly, so the Conventions batch is skewed toward boilerplate files instead of true local convention drift.
findings.suggestion: Change sibling grouping to a narrower boundary (for example immediate parent package or configurable depth), and filter out low-value boilerplate modules (`__init__.py`, tiny entrypoints) before emitting outliers for batch selection.

findings.summary: Duplicate-cluster context often loses file references, so duplicate leads are dropped from batch prompts.
evidence: Packet duplicate clusters are present but mostly contain `files: []`.
In `_build_duplicate_clusters`, empty `detail.files` is preserved as empty instead of using fallback file paths.
In `_batch_conventions_errors`, duplicate seeds are only derived from populated `files`, so empty clusters provide no files to investigate.
findings.suggestion: In duplicate-cluster construction, always emit at least one concrete file path (fallback to the finding’s own `file` or parsed member metadata), and in batch builder, add a secondary fallback to include that path when `files` is empty.

findings.summary: Plan-load failures are silently downgraded to `None` while adjacent state failures hard-exit.
evidence: Both command paths wrap `load_plan()` in broad `PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS` and continue with `_resolve_plan = None` / `_import_plan = None` without surfacing cause.
The same modules hard-fail on state/config persistence errors via explicit error output and `sys.exit(1)`.
This mixed boundary behavior makes equivalent operational failures (state/plan integrity) behave inconsistently and can hide plan corruption during command execution.
findings.suggestion: Standardize command-layer failure policy for plan I/O: either treat plan-load failure as actionable warning with explicit context every time, or fail fast for non-recoverable parse/data errors and reserve silent fallback only for narrowly defined transient cases.

findings.summary: Review batch pipeline drops new abstraction_fitness sub-axes before merge/import.
evidence: Prompt contract requires six abstraction sub-axes (`batch_prompt_template.py:227-237,348`).
Merge wiring passes only three keys via `ABSTRACTION_SUB_AXES` (`batch.py:40-49,105,255`).
Normalizer/aggregator loops only over provided axes (`batch_core.py:152-181,493-496,636-676`), so delegation/type-discipline metadata is silently discarded.
findings.suggestion: Expand `ABSTRACTION_SUB_AXES` and `ABSTRACTION_COMPONENT_NAMES` in `batch.py` to include `delegation_density`, `definition_directness`, and `type_discipline`, then keep merge/normalization keyed off that unified tuple so prompt/output/storage contracts stay aligned.

findings.summary: Per-file import can auto-resolve valid open findings from invalid replacement payloads.
evidence: Invalid dimensions are skipped (`per_file.py:98-107`) but their files still enter `valid_reviewed_files_rel` if required fields exist (`132-136`).
Auto-resolve uses that broad set (`170-183`), resolving old open findings for files that had no accepted replacement finding.
The shared resolver in `shared.py:226-245` trusts caller scope, so this mismatch directly affects state churn.
findings.suggestion: Build `reimported_files` from successfully imported findings (`review_findings`) only, or track a separate `accepted_files` set during validation and use that for `auto_resolve_review_findings`.

findings.summary: Review modules import siblings via package root, reinforcing avoidable import cycles.
evidence: `__init__.py` imports `entrypoint` (`__init__.py:3`), which imports `batch` (`entrypoint.py:12`).
`batch.py:11-13` then imports sibling modules through `desloppify.app.commands.review` package namespace, creating back-edges during package initialization.
Holistic dependency evidence reports this as an active cycle family in review command modules.
findings.suggestion: Replace intra-package imports that go through `desloppify.app.commands.review` with direct relative imports (for example, `from . import batch_core`) and keep `__init__.py` minimal to avoid pull-in during sibling module load.

findings.summary: Identical move wrapper modules add indirection while erasing signatures.
evidence: `dart/move.py` and `gdscript/move.py` are line-for-line identical wrappers forwarding `*args/**kwargs` to scaffold helpers.
No language-specific policy or translation is applied in these wrappers (`move.py:16-25` in both files).
Shared helper implementations already exist in `_framework/commands_base.py`, so duplicated adapter files only add call hops.
findings.suggestion: Either import scaffold helpers directly in each language command registration site or generate these adapters from one typed helper factory; avoid handwritten `*args/**kwargs` wrappers so signatures remain explicit.

findings.summary: Queue-cycle gating and frozen-score lifecycle paths lack direct tests
evidence: `queue_progress.py` drives score reveal/freeze behavior used by resolve/fix/review commands, but there are no direct tests targeting these functions.
`scan_preflight.py` contains the `--force-rescan` attestation gate and queue-blocking logic, but scan command tests monkeypatch preflight out (`test_cmd_scan.py` lines 51/140/218).
This leaves high-impact workflow controls unverified under realistic plan/state combinations.
findings.suggestion: Add focused tests for `queue_progress` and `scan_queue_preflight` covering: active plan with remaining queue, queue-drained reveal, `PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS` fallback, `--force-rescan` attestation acceptance/rejection, and plan_start_scores reset behavior.

findings.summary: Review batch flow duplicates coercion APIs instead of reusing shared contracts
evidence: `core/_internal/coercions.py` already provides `coerce_positive_int`, `coerce_positive_float`, and `coerce_non_negative_float`.
`review/batches.py` reimplements these functions locally with overlapping semantics.
In the same review subsystem, `review/prepare.py` uses the shared coercion helper while `review/batches.py` uses local copies, producing inconsistent API ownership and drift potential.
findings.suggestion: Remove local coercion helpers from `review/batches.py`, import shared coercions from `core/_internal/coercions.py`, and add any genuinely missing coercion variants to the shared module so one contract governs CLI/config coercion behavior.

findings.summary: Plan-load and queue-display choreography is duplicated across command boundaries
evidence: Multiple commands each perform their own `load_plan`/`PLAN_LOAD_EXCEPTIONS` handling before calling queue progress rendering.
The seam between plan persistence and output rendering is not encapsulated, so orchestration concerns leak into command handlers.
This repeated choreography increases integration entropy and makes queue lifecycle behavior harder to evolve consistently.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single helper (e.g., `resolve_plan_queue_context(state, state_path)`) that handles plan loading, exception fallback, remaining-queue computation, and render decision in one place; migrate command callers to that boundary.

findings.summary: Edge auth check can be bypassed by unrelated auth token usage elsewhere in file
evidence: `_file_level_security_findings` flags missing auth only when `_AUTH_CHECK_RE.search(content)` is false; this is whole-file matching, not per-handler matching.
A helper/import-level `auth.getUser` token anywhere in the file suppresses `edge_function_missing_auth` even if the served route path itself has no guard.
Existing tests cover missing-auth and valid-auth cases but not this false-negative bypass pattern.
findings.suggestion: Parse edge handler bodies (or at least handler-adjacent slices) and require auth checks inside each served handler path; add a regression test where a non-handler helper contains `auth.getUser` while the served handler is unauthenticated.

findings.summary: RLS coverage heuristics miss quoted/schema-qualified table definitions
evidence: `_RLS_TABLE_RE`/`_RLS_ENABLE_RE` in `context_signals/auth.py` capture only `\w+` identifiers.
The same simplified assumption appears in TS security RLS view checks, indicating a shared weak SQL-pattern approach.
Quoted identifiers or schema-qualified names are common in SQL and will be dropped from `rls_without`, reducing surfaced auth/RLS gaps.
findings.suggestion: Expand SQL matching to handle quoted and schema-qualified identifiers (or use a lightweight SQL parser) and add tests for `schema.table`, quoted names, and mixed-case identifiers.

findings.summary: Authorization batch generation discards computed RLS coverage signals
evidence: `gather_auth_context` returns `rls_coverage` in the authorization context payload.
`_batch_authorization` does not read `rls_coverage` when building `files_to_read`, despite batch rationale explicitly citing RLS coverage.
This is a seam break where upstream context production is not consumed by downstream batch selection.
findings.suggestion: Extend `_batch_authorization` to include representative SQL/migration files tied to `rls_coverage.without_rls` (and optionally contrasting `with_rls` peers) so generated RLS evidence reaches prompt execution.

findings.summary: Security hook migration is incomplete: runtime still supports legacy tuple and new result contracts.
evidence: `phase_security` in `shared_phases.py` branches between `LangSecurityResult` and legacy tuple unpacking.
`LangConfig` still defines tuple-based `detect_lang_security` and wraps it into `detect_lang_security_detailed` by default.
TypeScript and C# configs still override only `detect_lang_security`, keeping tuple compatibility active in normal runtime.
findings.suggestion: Complete migration to `detect_lang_security_detailed` for built-in languages, then remove tuple fallback from shared phase code behind a versioned cutoff; add a CI assertion that language plugins return `LangSecurityResult`.

findings.summary: TypeScript detectors expose both legacy tuple and new result APIs, prolonging migration debt.
evidence: Each detector defines `detect_*` returning tuple and `detect_*_result` returning `DetectorResult`; `deprecated.py` explicitly marks the tuple wrapper as temporary compatibility.
Core phases already consume `*_result` APIs, but other runtime entrypoints still call tuple wrappers (`detect_logs(path)[0]`, `detect_ts_security(files, zone_map)`).
findings.suggestion: Standardize detector internals on `DetectorResult` only, keep one centralized adapter for CLI/testing compatibility, and migrate runtime consumers (`__init__.py` and fixer detector hooks) to result-based helpers.

findings.summary: Next-command rendering combines too many responsibilities and silently suppresses runtime errors.
evidence: `_render_item` in `next_parts/render.py` mixes cluster rendering, subjective-card rendering, plan metadata, snippet extraction, impact estimation, and explain formatting in one long branch-heavy function.
Two exception blocks in `_render_item` swallow exceptions via `_ = exc`, making score/drag rendering failures invisible.
Same silent-catch idiom appears repeatedly in `review/runner_helpers.py`, indicating a broader local-error-handling style drift.
findings.suggestion: Split `_render_item` into dedicated render subroutines (cluster, subjective, mechanical finding, impact/explain), and replace `_ = exc` blocks with a shared non-fatal debug logger so degraded output remains diagnosable.

findings.summary: The scan package is a flat 17-file directory mixing lifecycle, reporting, and artifact concerns.
evidence: Directory profile reports 17 files in `desloppify/app/commands/scan/`, exceeding the >10 flat-directory overload heuristic for mixed concerns.

`scan.py` imports workflow (`prepare_scan_runtime`, `merge_scan_results`), artifacts (`build_scan_query_payload`, `emit_scorecard_badge`), and multiple reporting modules directly, indicating weak internal package boundaries.
`scan_artifacts.py` mixes query payload composition and badge emission/UI messaging, while reporting responsibilities are distributed across multiple sibling files.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `desloppify/app/commands/scan/lifecycle/` for runtime/merge/noise helpers and move `scan_workflow.py` there first; (2) create `desloppify/app/commands/scan/reporting/` and move `scan_reporting_*` files; (3) split `scan_artifacts.py` into `payload.py` and `badge.py` under `scan/artifacts/`; (4) keep `scan.py` as thin entrypoint. After each move run `desloppify move <src> <dst> --dry-run`, then apply move, then validate with `rg "app\.commands\.scan\.(scan_workflow|scan_reporting|scan_artifacts)" -n`, `pytest -q desloppify/tests/commands`, and `python -m desloppify --help`.

findings.summary: TypeScript smell detectors are split across overlapping modules with duplicated detector ownership.
evidence: `smells.py` defines local `_detect_empty_if_chains` and `_detect_dead_useeffects` and calls them in `detect_smells`.
`_smell_effects.py` defines `detect_empty_if_chains` and `detect_dead_useeffects`, and `_smell_helpers.py` wraps those functions again (`_detect_*_impl` indirection).

This creates three placement layers for the same smell family (caller in `smells.py`, wrappers in `_smell_helpers.py`, implementations in `_smell_effects.py`) plus `_smell_detectors.py` for related logic, so module ownership is ambiguous.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `desloppify/languages/typescript/detectors/smells/` with `core.py` (scan orchestration), `regex_rules.py`, and `multiline.py`; (2) move implementations from `_smell_effects.py` and `_smell_detectors.py` into `multiline.py`; (3) delete duplicate local implementations from `smells.py`; (4) keep one compatibility shim file for one release, then remove. Update imports with `desloppify move <src> <dst> --dry-run`, then run `rg "_smell_(effects|helpers|detectors)" -n`, `pytest -q desloppify/languages/typescript/tests/smells`, and a CLI smoke `desloppify scan --path . --lang typescript --skip-slow`.

findings.summary: Cycle-avoidance imports in scan entrypoints signal unresolved boundary direction issues.
evidence: `scan/__init__.py` exports `cmd_scan` via lazy `__getattr__` explicitly "to avoid import cycles."
`scan.py` performs function-level imports in `_print_plan_workflow_nudge` and guards with `ImportError`/`AttributeError`, another cycle workaround pattern.
`registry.py` already centralizes lazy command loading, so additional scan-local laziness indicates boundaries are not cleanly layered.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) move plan-progress nudge logic into a dedicated dependency-light module (e.g., `app/commands/scan/reporting/plan_nudge.py`) that depends only on `engine.plan` + queue helper contracts; (2) make `scan.py` import it normally at module load; (3) replace scan package `__getattr__` with explicit `from .scan import cmd_scan` once cycle is removed; (4) keep command lazy-loading only in `registry.py`. Validate by running `python -c "from desloppify.app.commands.scan import cmd_scan"`, `rg "__getattr__\(|from .* import .* as _load_plan_for_nudge" -n desloppify/app/commands/scan`, and `pytest -q desloppify/tests/commands`.

findings.summary: Language/detector registration relies on process-global mutable state with order-dependent behavior
evidence: `registry_state.py` keeps mutable module globals (`_registry`, `_load_attempted`, `_load_errors`) that are shared across all runs in-process.
`core/registry.py` mutates global detector catalogs in `register_detector()`; `generic.py` calls this at runtime for tool-defined detectors.
`discovery.load_all()` short-circuits on `was_load_attempted()`, so prior mutation history directly controls initialization behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce an explicit registry context object (language + detector state) owned by runtime/session and pass it through discovery/registration APIs; keep module-level globals only as a thin default singleton for backward compatibility.

findings.summary: Registry state API leaks and preserves stale init flags/errors across clears
evidence: `registry_state.clear()` only clears `_registry` and does not reset `_load_attempted` or `_load_errors`.
`get_load_errors()` returns the internal `_load_errors` dict by reference; external callers can mutate it out-of-band.
`set_load_errors(errors)` assigns caller-owned dict directly, so later caller mutations alter internal state.
Tests must manually call `set_load_attempted(False)` and `set_load_errors({})` in addition to `clear()`, showing `clear()` is not a coherent reset primitive.
findings.suggestion: Make `clear()` a full reset (`_registry`, `_load_attempted`, `_load_errors`), return copies from getters, and defensively copy inputs in setters to remove aliasing and boot-order leakage.

findings.summary: Complexity hotspot aggregation consumes a different data shape than structural producers emit
evidence: `mechanical.py` `_build_complexity_hotspots()` calls `_get_signals(finding)` and expects metrics under `detail['signals']`.
`merge_structural_signals()` writes structural metrics directly into `detail` (`loc`, `complexity_score`, etc.), not nested under `signals`.
Resulting packet evidence has many hotspot entries with zero metrics, confirming the contract mismatch is live and degrading downstream review guidance.
findings.suggestion: Standardize a single structural finding contract and update both producer and aggregator together (prefer a typed payload model); add a regression test asserting hotspot entries preserve non-zero metrics for known structural findings.

findings.summary: Rerun preflight gate runs before import/submit modes and can block backlog-draining imports
evidence: `entrypoint.py` calls `review_rerun_preflight()` before `do_import()` and before `do_external_submit()`.
`preflight.py` exits with code 1 when open objective or subjective backlog exists (unless `--force-review-rerun`), with message 'Blocked: rerun requires drained backlog'.
Import/submit are resolution steps that should reduce backlog; gating them behind already-drained backlog creates workflow starvation/churn risk.
findings.suggestion: Apply rerun preflight only to modes that start a new review cycle (`--prepare`, `--run-batches`, `--external-start`), and skip it for pure import/submit/validate paths; keep a separate import integrity validator for those modes.

findings.summary: Plan resolver bypasses declared public state API and imports private internals directly
evidence: `desloppify/state.py:3-5` states `engine._state` is internal and this module is the stable API.
`desloppify/app/commands/plan/_resolve.py:6-7` imports `desloppify.engine._state.resolution` and `desloppify.engine._state.schema` directly.
findings.suggestion: Route plan resolution through the public facade (`desloppify.state`) by exporting `match_findings`/`StateModel` usage there, then remove direct `_state` imports from command modules. Add an import-boundary test preventing `desloppify/app/commands/**` from importing `desloppify.engine._state.*`.

findings.summary: Prepare and run-batches paths duplicate packet policy logic instead of sharing one contract
evidence: Both modules implement near-identical `_redacted_review_config` and `_coerce_review_batch_file_limit` helpers.
Both assemble packet with `prepare_holistic_review`, append narrative, and compose `next_command` strings independently (`prepare.py:66-90`, `batch.py:159-182`).
findings.suggestion: Create a shared packet-assembly module (for example `desloppify/app/commands/review/packet_policy.py`) that owns config redaction, limits, and next-command generation; have both prepare and run-batches call that single builder so governance policy changes happen in one place.

findings.summary: Compatibility facades have planned-removal dates but no executable deprecation governance
evidence: Both files announce planned removal dates in docstrings (`2026-09-30`) while continuing full export surfaces.
No runtime warning, usage telemetry, or migration gate is implemented in these modules to enforce or monitor the sunset policy.
findings.suggestion: Implement staged deprecation governance: Phase 1 warn on import (opt-out env var for CI), Phase 2 emit usage counters to state/query output, Phase 3 fail in strict mode behind a config flag before final removal; document the dates in a single policy constant consumed by both modules.

findings.summary: Quality gates do not enforce key architecture contracts around state boundaries and packet policy
evidence: `test_import_boundaries.py` validates some layering rules but has no assertion preventing app commands from importing `engine._state` internals.
No test asserts that prepare and run-batches paths produce the same packet policy fields from one shared source; current behavior can drift silently.
findings.suggestion: Add contract tests: (1) AST import-boundary test for `desloppify/app/commands/**` blocking `desloppify.engine._state.*`; (2) golden test that both prepare-mode and run-batches packet creation paths produce equivalent policy fields (`config` redaction, `next_command`, batch cap) from a shared builder.

findings.summary: The review command package is overloaded, mixing policy, orchestration, and transport concerns
evidence: `holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles` shows `desloppify/app/commands/review/` with 16 files and 6954 LOC.
Directory imports span multiple concern domains (`core`, `helpers`, `intelligence/review`, `narrative`, `runtime`) rather than a tight command boundary.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 create folders `review/policy/`, `review/orchestration/`, `review/io/`, `review/modes/`; Stage 2 move packet/config/preflight policy modules first (`prepare.py` shared parts, preflight helpers) into `policy/`; Stage 3 move runner execution (`batch.py`, `batches.py`, `runner_helpers.py`) into `orchestration/`; Stage 4 move import/export plumbing (`import_cmd.py`, `import_helpers.py`, `external.py`) into `io/`; Stage 5 keep thin mode entry wrappers in `modes/` and update `entrypoint.py` imports. Validate with `rg -n "from \." desloppify/app/commands/review`, `pytest desloppify/tests/review -q`, and `pytest desloppify/tests/commands/test_review_preflight.py -q`.

evidence: Private module boundary is widely bypassed: `desloppify.core._internal.text_utils` is imported directly by app/engine/language modules (including seed files `app/commands/update_skill.py`, `app/commands/status_parts/render.py`, `languages/_framework/base/types.py`).

Review package has an import-cycle-prone shape: `app/commands/review/__init__.py` imports `entrypoint`, `entrypoint.py` imports `batch.py`, and `batch.py` imports submodules through `desloppify.app.commands.review` package-level imports.

Finding status policy is duplicated across many command renderers as raw strings (`"open"`, `"wontfix"`, etc.) even though `core/enums.py` defines canonical status tokens, increasing boundary drift risk.

Packet signal check: `holistic_context.coupling.boundary_violations` and `holistic_context.dependencies.deferred_import_density` are null/missing in this packet, so boundary validation required direct code verification.

Workflow policy currently combines two starvation pressures: rerun preflight blocks review reruns whenever objective backlog exists (`app/commands/review/preflight.py`), while queue ranking forces subjective items to T4 behind mechanical work (`engine/_work_queue/ranking.py`).
This creates a repeated path where target-improving subjective work can be deferred indefinitely unless users manually override with `--force-review-rerun`, which is a weak operational guardrail.
Queue/render messaging acknowledges this policy (`app/commands/next_parts/render.py`) but does not add automatic fairness gates or periodic subjective review windows.

Confirmed mechanical signal in `app/commands/review/prepare.py`: `do_prepare` mixes packet assembly, narrative computation, command synthesis, CLI UX rendering, and query persistence in one 150+ LOC function (lines 37-191).

Confirmed mechanical signal in `app/commands/plan/override_handlers.py`: skip/unskip/reopen handlers each orchestrate state + plan mutations directly, repeating similar coordination logic across commands (lines 77-229).

Confirmed structural duplication: review finding-merge semantics are duplicated in two modules (`app/commands/review/merge.py` `_merge_finding_details` lines 81-113 and `app/commands/review/batch_core.py` `_merge_finding_payload` lines 525-560), increasing drift risk.

Workflow-integrity risk: `review/merge.py` groups duplicates with a greedy one-pass candidate scan (`consumed` set, lines 143-163), which can miss transitive duplicates and leave reopen/merge churn in later runs.

Refuted mechanical hypothesis for `scan_reporting_summary.py` swallowed errors: catch in `show_concern_count` is explicitly best-effort optional reporting; core scan flow is not suppressed (lines 219-233).

Refuted mechanical hypothesis for `status_cmd.py` complexity as major defect: `cmd_status` is mostly a top-level orchestrator that delegates rendering to `status_parts.render`; complexity is high but boundary remains mostly honest (lines 48-181).
Confirmed mutable-global coupling from scan evidence: `languages/_framework/registry_state.py` keeps module-level `_registry`, `_load_attempted`, and `_load_errors` mutable state (lines 26-28).
`registry_state.clear()` clears only `_registry` and leaves `_load_attempted`/`_load_errors` untouched (lines 64-66), so state reset is partial and order-dependent.
`languages/_framework/discovery.py` `load_all()` short-circuits permanently once `was_load_attempted()` is true (lines 49-51), so plugin discovery cannot refresh in-process after first attempt.

`languages/_framework/resolution.py` repeatedly calls `load_all()` for `get_lang`, `auto_detect_lang`, and `available_langs` (lines 34, 51, 100), making behavior dependent on prior initialization lifecycle.

Additional global-coupling hotspot: `core/registry.py` mutates global detector registries/callbacks at runtime (lines 329-347), while `cli.py` caches `_DETECTOR_NAMES` once (lines 24-36) without registry callback invalidation, creating init-order cache staleness risk.

Naming across the reviewed batch is mostly explicit, but API-facade naming is inconsistent in intent signaling (`core/discovery_api.py`, `core/output_api.py`, `state.py`) because some modules are pure re-export facades while others in the same layer expose behavior and policy.

Mechanical `complexity_hotspots` entries for `app/commands/next_parts/render.py` and `app/commands/status_parts/render.py` did not surface naming-specific defects beyond minor verbosity; names are generally descriptive in those hotspots.
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: Facade/API modules use mixed naming conventions for equivalent responsibilities (barrel export vs behavioral API), which makes import intent less predictable.

evidence: `app/commands/review/merge.py` treats two findings as the same issue immediately when parsed holistic identifier tokens match (`_same_issue_concept`, lines 63-66), before checking summary similarity or related-file overlap.

Holistic payload validation (`intelligence/review/importing/contracts.py`) requires non-empty `identifier` but does not enforce uniqueness, so repeated identifiers can trigger unintended merges and `auto_resolved` state updates for distinct findings.
Mechanical complexity hotspot signal for `app/commands/next_parts/render.py` was inspected and mostly refuted as a direct logic defect (large function, but branches are behaviorally distinct).

`intelligence/review/importing/shared.py` computes `score = max(0, min(100, score))` (line 60) without numeric coercion/validation when `assessments` values are dict payloads; a non-numeric `score` value raises `TypeError` at runtime.

`app/commands/review/import_cmd.py` accepts imported payloads and calls `import_holistic_findings(...)` directly; parse/contract checks only enforce `assessments` is an object, not that `score` fields are numeric, so malformed payloads can crash import execution.
This confirms a high-signal import-path weakness adjacent to the packet’s `signal_density` leads in review import modules.

`core/_internal/text_utils.py` documents `get_project_root()` as runtime-context aware, but `read_code_snippet(..., project_root=None)` still defaults to module-level `PROJECT_ROOT` snapshot (line 41) instead of the runtime-aware getter.

`core/config.py` builds `CONFIG_FILE` from imported `PROJECT_ROOT` at import time (line 16), so default `load_config()` / `save_config()` path resolution is not aligned with runtime root overrides used elsewhere (`core/source_discovery.py`, `core/paths_api.py`).
This is a structural contract split: some modules honor `RuntimeContext.project_root`, others pin to import-time constants, producing inconsistent path semantics across command/runtime flows.
Reviewed paths usually use explicit CLI exits for policy errors and preserve user-facing context.
In `batches.py`, non-`SystemExit` import failures are logged and re-raised generically, which preserves traceback but yields uneven typed error handling across similar command boundaries.
error_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Exception strategy is mostly coherent but not uniformly typed across review orchestration layers.

evidence: `app/commands/review/batches.py` reimplements policy parsing/coercion and runtime calculations that also exist in `app/commands/review/runtime/policy.py`.
This duplicated abstraction forces two policy surfaces to stay synchronized and raises drift risk.
Holistic abstraction signals also show elevated wrapper/indirection pressure in review orchestration hotspots.
Most reviewed code avoids verbose restating comments and keeps operational comments purpose-driven.
However, some orchestration helpers still show repetitive boilerplate patterns (similar coercion/logging blocks repeated across modules).
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: Boilerplate repetition in review command helpers still inflates code volume without adding policy value.

evidence: Hand-offs between `entrypoint -> preflight -> batches/import` are explicit.
Integration seams are heavier than needed because policy resolution and execution concerns are partially duplicated across modules.
mid_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: Boundary choreography still requires cross-reading multiple large helpers for one execution path.

evidence: Smaller modules (`helpers.py`, `runtime/policy.py`) are locally clean and direct.
Very large files (`runner_helpers.py`, `batches.py`, `import_helpers.py`) reduce local precision via long multipurpose internals.
Naming and module conventions are largely consistent within reviewed command paths.
Mechanical duplicate-cluster metadata is present but lacked actionable file membership in this packet, so no strong convention drift was confirmed from that signal.
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: Some convention checks are under-instrumented (cluster entries without file payload), limiting enforceability.

evidence: Reviewed files rely primarily on internal modules and stdlib; no clear third-party sprawl was found in this batch scope.
Security/dependency hotspots were reported elsewhere, but not substantiated in these seed command modules during this pass.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Health is good in reviewed scope, but hotspot evidence exists outside seed files and should be tracked in dedicated dependency/security sweeps.

evidence: Holistic testing signals list critical untested modules including `app/commands/helpers/queue_progress.py` and `core/output.py` with high importer counts.
Repository test search found no direct tests targeting queue-progress helpers (`plan_aware_queue_count`, `print_execution_or_reveal`) despite their control-flow role in user guidance.
CLI review mode selection is explicit and mutually exclusive in `entrypoint.py`.
API surface remains broad in review package; closely related responsibilities are exposed across many modules rather than a narrower facade.
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: Review APIs are coherent but too spread out for low-friction maintenance.

evidence: External submit path enforces session id/token matching and injects canonical provenance before import.
Attested external mode is guarded by required attestation phrases and runner restriction checks.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Trust and provenance checks are robust but distributed across multiple modules, which raises policy-fragmentation risk.

evidence: Migration signals in this packet are limited (deprecated markers mostly outside reviewed command files).

Mechanical dead-code flagged `.github/scripts/roslyn_stub.py` as orphaned, but this is refuted by active workflow/test references (`.github/workflows/integration.yml`, `tests/ci/test_ci_contracts.py`).
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: Some migration/dead-code detectors still emit low-precision hits, adding cleanup noise.

evidence: `desloppify/app/commands/review/` is an overloaded package (16 files, ~6954 LOC) with mixed concerns: orchestration, trust policy, external session lifecycle, import normalization, scoring, and rendering.
Largest files in one package (`runner_helpers.py` 1512 LOC, `import_helpers.py` 981 LOC, `batches.py` 880 LOC) indicate concern flattening rather than bounded subpackages.
